evidence_quotes.quote: The game was sold as an "Online-Only" singleplayer racer with a standalone multiplayer lobby feature.
purchases as a limited license instead of an actual purchase of an product
companies has mislead consumers into believing they have bought a videogame or a console, which they can take away from the consumer whenever they feel like.

paragraphs.text: Hello. In this day and age you see more often companies treat purchases as a limited license instead of an actual purchase of an product, which in this case is videogames. As a person which loves game both new and old, can not stand for what companies are doing in this day and age. A game i bought as a physical media for the Playstation 4, made by SONY in 2014 named "The Crew", published by Ubisoft was shut down 30th september 2024 by the publisher. The game was sold as an "Online-Only" singleplayer racer with a standalone multiplayer lobby feature. After the server shutdowns there is no longer a way to play the game in any "legal" ways or any support for the Playstation 4. Because of that, my physical copy is now nothing more than a nostalgic paperweight. Over 20 years, i have played countless of games on several consoles and on the Computer. From the beginning if you bought a videogame, it was your videogame until consoles had access to the internet. After the 7th generation of videogame consoles (estimated to be between the years 20052017) games and game consoles have become more and more online dependent. Physical videogames for the console PS4 (Playstation 4) works on the patent where the physical Video game disc do not contain the whole game, but a fraction where you must download the rest of the game while starting it for the first time. This means we are now dependent that Sony will keep supporting the PS4 in the future, or else people's physical medias (which for some people can be worth thousands of euro) will be rendered useless. In summary, for almost 20 years as of writing this feedback, companies has mislead consumers into believing they have bought a videogame or a console, which they can take away from the consumer whenever they feel like. Some ways to fix problem for us consumers, has been suggested by the "Stop Killing Games" movement (e.g "The SKG initiative proposes that games at risk of being discontinued (e.g. taking down servers after ending support) should have a clear end-of-life plan to make it possible to retain access to them - which is arguably a basic consumer right.") Thank you for your patience.
